text: The robotic system is used to perform a preperitoneal repair of a large Spigelian hernia, with visible manipulation of tissues in the presence of a large abdominal aortic aneurysm.
Disclosures include intuitive, barred, and gore.
The patient is an eighty-two-year-old male with a large, left-sided, incarcerated spagalian hernia that was causing him significant discomfort.
His past medical history was significant for non-insulin-dependent diabetes, stage three chronic kidney disease, and atrial fibrillation for which he took Xarelto.
His surgical history was significant for an open right hemicolectomy for a benign polyp, as well as an endovascular aneurysm repair for an eight-centimeter abdominal aortic aneurysm.
that had a known type II endoleak.
He also had a remote smoking history and was a daily drinker.
Images display the altered contour of the patient's abdominal wall due to the presence of a large, left-sided, incarcerated Spigelian hernia.
His preoperative CT shows the previous extensive EVAR stent as well as a large left-sided Spigalian hernia containing loops of bowel without evidence of bowel compromise.
The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum using a Veress needle in the left upper quadrant, followed by the insertion of a 12mm trocar with the OptiView technique.
An eight millimeter trocar is placed in the right upper quadrant, followed by the placement of two additional trocars in the right mid abdomen.
The surgeon performs an extended lysis of adhesions to expose the left Spigalian hernia defect, carefully separating and clearing adhesions to improve visibility of the anatomical area.
The sigmoid colon is visibly adherent to the hernia sac, preventing easy reduction of the herniated tissue.
The surgeon performs a transabdominal preperitoneal repair, accessing the abdominal cavity to address the left-sided Spigalian hernia.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum in the left lower quadrant, entering the preperitoneal plane to continue the surgical repair.
The surgeon divides the preperitoneal attachments of the hernia sac and successfully reduces the entire hernia sac.
A large abdominal aortic aneurysm is visibly pulsating in the foreground of the surgical field.
increasing the difficulty in visualization and dissection throughout the case.
An assistant port is used to retract the redundant hernia sac and its contents, facilitating the division of the most ventral attachments within the surgical field.
The surgeon develops the pre-peritoneal space, expanding it to accommodate the anticipated size of the mesh implant.
The surgeon carefully dissects the preperitoneal space, ensuring the identification and protection of the spermatic cord and inferior epigastric vessels.

Following the partial closure of the defect with an O V-lock suture, the surgeon makes a stab incision at the center of the defect. A seven French flat JP drain is then placed in the space previously occupied by the hernia sac, situated between the external and internal oblique muscles.
The surgical defect is measured to be three by five centimeters.
The surgeon completes the transverse closure of the defect using an OV-lock suture. A fifteen-centimeter circular piece of Ventralite ST echo mesh is selected and prepared to reinforce the repair.

The surgeon introduces and unrolls the Ventralite ST echo mesh into the preperitoneal space. A suture passer is used to pull up on the central cord of the mesh, positioning it for reinforcement of the defect.
The surgeon inflates the structural mesh balloon, expanding the preperitoneal space to accommodate the mesh circumferentially.
The surgeon uses TuoVigril sutures to secure the mesh in multiple locations, ensuring it is firmly anchored within the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon carefully places sutures, avoiding the triangle of pain lateral to the spermatic cord, and ensures the sutures follow the anticipated trajectory of the nerves without impinging on them.
The surgeon removes the positioning balloon from the surgical site, revealing the underlying mesh and repaired area.
The mesh is positioned flat against the abdominal wall, with no visible redundancies or folds, indicating proper placement.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap using a V-lock suture, ensuring the flap is securely sealed over the mesh.
The surgeon over-sews any peritoneal defects in the closure, completing the repair of the surgical site.

His postoperative course was prolonged, secondary to development of a large preperitoneal hematoma after resuming his Xarelto, highlighted by the red star on this representative cut of a postoperative CT.
He also struggled with insomnia and delirium, resulting
in early inadvertent removal of his JP drain and subsequent intramuscular seroma formation highlighted by the yellow star.
Finally, he suffered a massive upper GI bleed from a duodenal ulcer that required multiple rounds of transfusion as well as multiple endoscopic interventions to control.
This also occurred once his Xarelto was again resumed.
Given his recurrent bleeding complications, it was deemed that the risks outweighed the benefits, and his Xarelta was stopped.
Despite his prolonged course, he recovered fully from his complications.
Watchful waiting was pursued for his hematoma and seroma, and interventional radiology is planning angioembolization for the type II endoleak.
